docs.title: Welcome to Scratch Script
docs.text:
Scratch script is a domain specific language that is made to compile to a scratch .sb3 file which can be imported into the scratch website.

Scratch script's syntax looks similar to the java programming language, however many of thier concepts are derived from the C programming language.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
The following is an example .scratch file that makes the cat step through the items in a list:

sprite Stage{}

sprite cat{
    costume catCostume "cat.svg";
    func main(){
        list myList = [10, 20, 30];
        for (ptr i = 0; i < myList.length; i += 1){
            move(myList[i]);
            sleep(1);
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Structure of Scratch Script
docs.text: Scratch Script is created to embody the functionality of base Scratch, so there must be some way to implement sprites.

docs.title: Sprites
docs.text:
You can create sprites by using the sprite keyword, similar to class in other languages.

sprite Stage{}

sprite cat{}

sprite thing{}

The text that comes after sprite will be the name of the sprite.

For example the sprite created on line 3 would be called 'cat'.

If the sprite name is Stage, the sprite is the stage and will have all of it's functionality. The stage does not have position, and variables created in them are accessable from other sprites.

docs.title: Contents of sprites
docs.text:
Inside a sprite, you can start creating blocks and functions.

sprite cat{
    move(10);

    func doSomething(){
        move(10);
    }

    func main(){
        doSomething();
    }
}

For more information, go to Scripts

docs.title: Costumes
docs.text:
Inside of the contents of a sprite, you can define a costume of a sprite by using the costume keyword.

The statement takes in two manditory parameters, and two optional ones.

The two manditory parameters are:

  • The name of the costume to be created
  • The relative path to the costume

The two optional parameters that come after the manditory ones, seperated by a comma:

  • The x offset of the image
  • The y offset of the image

Offsets are optional and the compiler will attempt to auto center the images if you do not specify an offset.

sprite cat{
    costume myCostumeName "foldername/filename.svg";

    costume myCostumeName2 "../myFile.png" 10, 3;
}

The first costume declaration creates a costume named myCostumeName with the file being at "foldername/filename.svg".

The second costume declaration creates a costume named myCostumeName2 with the file being at "../myFile.png" with a visual offset of 10, 3.

Notice: raster images are destructive and anything outside of the bounds of the screen will be clipped when importing.

Currently the only file extensions implamented are svg, png, jpg.

docs.title: Importing Scripts
docs.text: Similarly to the #include keyword in C, the import keyword in Scratch Script "runs" the file specified ""linking"" the files together. In actuality, this process is achieved by different means in both cases.

docs.title: Import
docs.text:
The import keyword can be used in two contexts:

  1. Within a script

  2. Not within a script

docs.title: Importing within a script
docs.text:
If the import keyword is used within the body of a sprite, the import will look for the export keyword within the .scratch file.

Once it has found the export keyword, it will run all of the commands within it.

To assign stacks, the original stack will "jump" over the import and will continue on without it.

main.scratch
sprite cat {
    move(4);
    import "otherfile.scratch";
    move(3);
}
otherfile.scratch
export {
    move(7);
}

The resulting stacks would be:

move(4); move(3);

and...

move(7);

docs.title: Importing outside of a script
docs.text:
If the import keyword is used outside of the script of a sprite, the import will look for all sprites within the .scratch file.

main.scratch
import "otherfile.scratch";

sprite cat {
    def main(){
        move(3);
    }
}
otherfile.scratch
sprite cat2 {
    move(7);
}

The resulting Scratch project would contain both cat and cat2.

This achieves the same thing as if you put the sprite in the base project.

docs.title: Require
docs.text:
The require keyword is a one time import.

If the .scratch file has not been imported yet, it imports it.

If the .scratch file has been imported before, it does not import the file.

main.scratch
sprite cat{
    require "otherfile.scratch";
    require "otherfile.scratch";
}

The resulting cat sprite would only have one coppy of the export of the "otherfile.scratch".

docs.title: File Path
docs.text:
The file in quotes for the require and import command is a relative file path, meaning you are able to put the file you want to import into a subdirectory.

For example a directory like this:

.
└─ project/
    ├─ main.scratch
    └─ subforlder/
        └─ example.scratch

Could have a import like this:

main.scratch
import "subfolder/example.scratch";

docs.title: Standard Packages
docs.text:
Standard Packages are files that are part of compiler and are used for base functionality such as pointers.

Importing standard packages are the exact same as importing regular .scratch files.

import "std.scratch";

This is required for some functionality.

docs.title: The Installation of Scratch script
docs.text:
For ease of use, there are install scripts.

If I am being honest, these shell and bash scripts were made by AI. The should work, but you should always read the install scripts. You can find the source code here for windows, and here for linux

The windows build binarys currently do not work.

Because I am using Nuitka to compile a python project into a standalone file, the code gets obfuscated and Windows throws a tantrum; assuming whatever I am doing is malicious. Until I get zip projects to work with github workflows, if you use windows, you will just have to use python to run the compiler.

Cheers, The Scratch Script Development Team.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vortech11/Comp-to-Scratch/main/install.sh | bash

docs.title: Running the compiler
docs.text:
After using the install script, you can use the following command:

scratch myProject.scratch

And obviously replace the "myProject.scratch" with the file you want to compile

docs.title: Using python
docs.text:
The compiler is able to be used using only python. By installing the repo, you are able to use the compiler without the binary.

git clone https://github.com/vortech11/Comp-to-Scratch.git

Check that you have python installed (around version 3.13, idk)

python --version

You can now compile .scratch files by running:

python "Comp-to-Scratch-path/main.py" "fileName"

This should result in an build folder being created in the directory of the .scratch file

For any future use of the compiler, you can always use

python "Comp-to-Scratch-path/main.py"

in place of

scratch

docs.title: Compiling yourself
docs.text:
If you want, you can even compile the program yourself. You can achieve this by using the nuitka python compiler.

You can read up on how to install Nuitka yourself here

To use Nuitka, you can run the following:

python -m nuitka main.py

Make sure you are running this while your current working directory is the same one containing main.py

docs.title: Using the compiler
docs.text:
For a list of flags and commands you can use the compiler with, run:

scratch --help

To compile a .scratch file, run

scratch "myPath/myFile.scratch"

Ability to run with a button

If you use vscode, you can use vscode extensions such as "Code Runner" to add a button to .scratch files and make them run the command

docs.title: Quirks of the compiler
docs.text:
As of version 2.0.0, The quality of the syntax is durastically improved.

However, it could still be useful to take note of the syntax at times.

docs.title: Whitespace
docs.text:
All whitespace is allowed.

sprite cat{
        script         [
start    (  )     ;
      move () ;
  if   (1  == 1 )  { move
  (3)}
]
    }

It looks horrible, but it works

docs.title: Brackets
docs.text:
All types of brackets are interchangeable*

Brackets include:

  • brackets: []

  • curly brackets: {}

  • parenthesis: ()

sprite cat(
    script{
        start(};
        move{3);
    ]
}

docs.title: Strings
docs.text:
There are no strings

What double quotes ("") do is include everything inside of it as one token.

This is useful for hex values

pen_setPenColorToColor("#ffffff");

This is also important for decimal values

move("10.3");

If you do not have double quotes with decimal or hex values, the compiler will throw an error

docs.title: Semicolons
docs.text:
Semicolons are relatively normal except for the fact that they must come after every function and command, excluding curly brackets.

This does not work

if (5 == 3){
    move(10);
};
move(3);

This does not work

if (5 == 3)(
    move(10);
)
move(3);

Instead it should look something like this:

if (5 == 3){
    move(10);
}
move(3);

...or this

if (5 == 3)(
    move(10);
);
move(3);

docs.title: Sprite Scripts
docs.text: The scripts of a sprite are the meat and potatoes of a project. Each statement roughly equates to one scratch blocks that gets created on compile.

docs.title: Stacking
docs.text:
Scratch blocks stack on top of one another. In Scratch Script, this happens automatically when there are two or more statments. 

move(3);
move(4);

This would equate to two stacks that both are ran when the green flag is clicked. One stack with a move by 3 and another with a move by 4.

docs.title: Opcodes
docs.text:
A Scratch Opcode is a specific identifier for a block. In scratch script, you can create any scratch block by calling the opcode as a function.

motion_movesteps(10);

looks_say("hi");

event_whenflagclicked();

A list of all opcodes used in Scratch Script can be found here

While this does work as the backend for most blocks, this is not the intended way of interacting with Scratch Script

docs.title: Macros
docs.text:
Built into Scratch Script is a set of macros that maps opcodes to more common place names

For example:

  • motion_movesteps has been renamed to move

  • looks_say to say

  • operator_round to round

Both the alias and the original are allowed, but macros are meant to be easier to remember.

move(10);

say("hi");

if(10 == 10){
    move(10);
}

A list of all aliases can be found here

docs.title: Entry Points
docs.text:
While stacking blocks on empty space is fun, placing them on something is better.

There are two types of entry points,

  • User defined functions

  • Scratch defined 'cap blocks'

Both types of entry points use the func keyword.

The difference is the name of the function declared.

def main(){
    move(3);
}

def doThing(){

}

The main function declared is a scratch defined entry point that aliases to the "when flag clicked" block.

You can have multiple main functions. They will all run asyncranicely. Good luck getting async to work tho.

User defined function names can be anything else that is not is a scratch defined entry point.

docs.title: Data
docs.text: Currently there are three types of data within Scratch Script: the var data type, list data type, and pointer data type.

docs.title: Vars
docs.text:
The var data type is the built-in scratch variable data type. Vars are created on compile time, or in other words, cannot be created after it is compiled.

To create the var data type you can run the following:

var varName;

This will initiate the variable (varName) with the value "": an empty string. To specify a different value the following can be ran:

var varName = 10;

You can set the value of the variable by using the = keyword.

varName = 10;

You can then apply operations with the variable by using the += keyword.

varName += 10;

These operations change the value of the variable (varName) by a value (10).

To get the value of a var, you can use the name of the variable in an expression.

var i = 3;
move(i);
move(i + 2);

In action this could look like:

var i;
i = 10;
i += 2;
move(i);

docs.title: Lists
docs.text:
By using the list keyword, a Scratch list is created similarly to a variable. It is created on compile time and cannot be created or deleted while the Scratch Project is running.

To create a list, use the list keyword:

list listName;
list listName = [];

Both of these scripts creates an empty list called listName that looks like this: []. I don't know which one is better so I will keep both.

If you want to initiate values or set them, you can simply use commas to deliminate different values at indexes.

list listName = [10, 4, 6, 7, 2];
listName = [3, 1];

Both of these lines create 'delete all of list' and 'add item to list' blocks, which means that they do not update the default state of the list.

The difference between the two is that the first one creates a list to be used.

To change a specific value at an index, you are able to do list indexing by doing the folloing:

list listName = [10, 3];
listName[2] = 5;

Base Scratch is 0st indexed compared to base scratch

In Scratch Script: 

listName = [10, 20]
listName[0] = 30
listName[1] = 40
//listName [30, 40]

In normal Scratch: 

list listName = [10, 20];
listName[1] = 30;
listName[2] = 40;
//listName [30, 40]

Attempting to change or get the 0th item does nothing.

To get the value at an index you can index through it as if you were to modify it, but within an expression.

list listName = [10];
move(listName[1]);

docs.title: Pointers
docs.text:
Similarly to the C programming language, pointers are variables that point to positions in memory. In scratch this "memory" is a set of lists.

There are two types of pointers in Scratch Script: * Smart pointers (garbage collected) * Dumb pointers (not garbage collected)

The major difference between normal Scratch variables and pointers, is that pointers are created at runtime and can be deleted.

To use pointers, there must be a require "std.scratch"; line before the use of pointers

To learn more about the import and require keywords, go to the Importing page.

To make a smart pointer, use the ptr keyword.

ptr varName;
ptr varName3 = 10;

You can modify and asign values.

ptr varName;
varName = 10;
varName += 3;

To get the value of a static var

ptr varName = 3;
move(varName);

To make a dumb pointer, use the dptr keyword.

Everything a smart pointer can do, a dumb pointer can do.

Unlike smart pointers, you also have to manualy garbage collect them.

You can delete a dumb pointer by using the del keyword.

dptr varName = 3;
move(varName);
del varName;

This automatically happens with a smart pointer.

Deleting dumb pointers is important

If you do not delete a dumb pointer, errors can pop up in the console.

loop(3){
    dptr distance = 10;
    move(distance);
}

// Variable `distance` already exists

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
Functions are part of base scratch and can be implamented by using the func keyword.

func funcName(){

}

A function can have inputs parameters.

Functions can have as many inputs as you want and are deliminated by commas.

func funcName(input1, input2){

}

You can then place statements within the function. Statements inside of a function can use inputs.

func funcName(input1, input2){
    if (input2){
        move(input1);
    }
}

Any smart pointers created in functions are deleted at the end of the function.

func funcName(){
    static varName = 3;
    move(varName);
}

// Var `varName` no longer exist.

docs.title: Function Returns
docs.text:
Functions in base scratch are not able to return values.

However, by the use of pointers, it is possible to pas in a reference to a pointer for it to be set inside of the function for it to be used outside of the function.

To use function returns, a function must return something.

To set a pointer to the function return, the << keyword is used.

func add(num1, num2){
    return num1 + num2;
}

ptr result << add(1, 2);
say(result);

You can create a pointer for the function return assignment, or you can use a pre-existing pointer.

The portion on the right side of the << must be a singular function call with nothing more.
